turns.text: Hello Basilia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It feels a bit strange to talk about it, honestly. Most people in Holden just see the bump and offer a polite, tight-lipped smile. They don't really *see* the rest of it.

I spend so much time reading about lung development and tracking growth charts that I think I could probably pass a medical board exam, but that’s just my way of keeping the panic at bay. When I’m in the clinic, the midwife is lovely, truly—she tells me my blood pressure is textbook and the baby’s heart rate is perfect. And I know I should be relieved. I *am* relieved. But hearing "perfect" while I’m sitting there alone in that paper-thin gown makes me feel like I’m disappearing. 

I’m twenty-one, and I’m doing this solo. Sometimes I feel so capable, especially when I feel those little flutters—that's the only time it feels like just the two of us against the world, and that’s a beautiful thing. But then I get home to a quiet apartment, and I start thinking about the labor room, and the silence there is terrifying. I just wish someone would ask me how I’m *doing*, not just how my vitals are. I’m trying so hard to build a life, but it feels like walking on glass.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

To be honest, I think I expected them to be... warmer? In my head, I pictured someone pulling up a chair, looking me in the eye, and maybe acknowledging that this was a big, scary thing to be doing by myself. I suppose I wanted someone to act like they were invested in me as a person, not just the pregnancy. I’d seen so many movies where the doctor is this mentor figure who holds your hand through the anxiety.

The reality was a lot more sterile. The office here in Holden is clean and efficient, but it’s fast. You walk in, you check a box, you get your weight and blood pressure checked, and then it’s a quick “heartbeat sounds great, see you in four weeks.” It’s all very clinical—lots of data points and charts. They aren't unkind, not at all, but I’m just another name on a clipboard between their ten-o’clock and their eleven-o’clock. 

I’ve had to teach myself to keep my mouth shut about the fear because they’re busy, and my “numbers” look good, so what is there to talk about? I wanted a partner in this, I guess—someone to help me navigate the "what-ifs." Instead, I just feel like I’m moving through a conveyor belt. It’s efficient, but it feels lonely.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—and I hate to admit this—was how invisible I felt. I think I expected that when I walked in, the sheer weight of being twenty-one and unmarried would make someone pause. I wanted them to see the uncertainty in my eyes, but they were so focused on my BMI and my lab results that my actual life felt completely peripheral.

The negative side of that, I suppose, is how isolating it is to be told "you're doing great" when you're actually struggling to sleep or wondering how you’ll afford everything. It’s like they’re grading me on a test I’m passing, but nobody is checking to see if I’m actually okay.

But if I’m being honest, the positive surprise was the ultrasound. Seeing that little flicker on the screen for the first time... that was the one moment where the room felt different. Even with the technician just checking off her boxes, the baby was so real and so active. In that instant, I didn't feel like a data point anymore. I felt like a mother. It was the first time I realized that even if I’m the only one in the room, I’m not actually alone. It’s a strange, bittersweet kind of strength, but it’s the only thing that keeps me going.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I think I expected a conversation. You know, like in the books, where the doctor or the midwife sits down with you, points to the screen, and explains things in plain language. I expected them to anticipate my questions—or at least leave space for them—rather than just rushing to the next patient. I thought there would be a moment where they’d say, "This is what we're looking for, and this is what it means for you and the baby."

The reality is... digital. Everything shows up on this portal app on my phone before I even get a call. I’ll get a notification, and there are all these acronyms and numbers. I spend hours Googling the terms, trying to decipher if a level is "normal" or if I should be panicked. When I finally do talk to someone, it’s usually a quick, "Everything looks normal, no issues," and then they’re gone. 

It feels like they’re managing a process, not a person. If I have a specific question about what a reading means for my specific situation—for the baby’s health or for my own peace of mind—it feels like I’m being a bother. I’m left to piece together the health of my own child through an app, and honestly, that just makes the "what-ifs" grow louder in the dark.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

There is a massive gap. It’s like they’re speaking a language that they assume I already understand, or perhaps they think I don’t *need* to understand it as long as the news is "good." 

When they tell me everything is "normal," that’s the end of the conversation for them. But for me, that’s just the beginning. I want to know why it’s normal, what could change, and how that’s impacting the little person growing inside me. When I try to ask those deeper, more "what if" questions, I get these vague, breezy answers. They treat my anxiety like it’s just a symptom of my age or my hormones, but it’s not. It’s a symptom of being the only person responsible for another life. 

I feel like I’m constantly hunting for information in the margins. I shouldn’t have to spend my nights obsessively researching clinical studies just to feel like I’m keeping up with my own pregnancy. I don't need a medical degree, but I do need someone to bridge the distance between those cold, automated lab reports and the reality of my daily life. It’s the difference between being a parent and being a patient, and right now, I feel like I’m barely being treated as either.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I really wanted—and maybe naively expected—the kind of relationship where I could walk in and not have to re-introduce myself. I thought I’d have one person who knew I was doing this on my own, who knew that my support system is practically non-existent, and who wouldn’t just look at my chart every single time like I’m a stranger. I wanted someone who would recognize the look on my face when I’m scared, not just the number on the scale.

The reality, though, is that I see whoever is on rotation. It’s like a revolving door. One week it’s a midwife who is kind but hurried, the next it’s a nurse practitioner who clearly hasn’t read the notes from my last visit. Every time I go in, I feel like I’m starting from square one. I have to summarize my story, mention that I’m flying solo, and try to establish some kind of rapport in the five minutes before they move on to the next patient. 

It makes me feel so exposed. It’s exhausting to have to advocate for myself and explain my situation over and over again to people who are just going to swap out next month. I’m not asking for a friendship, just a little bit of recognition. I just want to feel like, for once, I’m not a stranger in my own pregnancy.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

Practically, it feels like I’m constantly losing ground. Every time I see a new face, I have to re-explain my concerns—my worries about work, the fact that I don’t have a partner to help with the physical strain, or even just my history. It’s like being a broken record. By the time I’ve caught them up to speed, the appointment is essentially over. 

Emotionally? It’s lonely. There’s no one to track the *me* that’s changing, only the clinical data points that are changing. I’ve started to feel like I have to "perform" during these visits. I put on my best, most composed face because I’m terrified that if I show them how overwhelmed I really am, they’ll just dismiss it as "first-time mom nerves" or, worse, something they aren’t equipped to handle. 

When you see different people, you stop trusting that anyone is actually looking out for the big picture. I find myself holding back questions because I don’t want to go through the whole explanation with someone who might not even be there for my next visit. It’s a very solitary way to go through something so monumental. I’m carrying the weight of the future by myself, and the healthcare system, for all its technical skill, feels like it's just letting me carry it alone.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I suppose I expected it to be a bit more... human? I thought there would be a balance between checking the technical stuff—you know, the measuring tape, the Doppler, the weight—and acknowledging the actual physical toll this takes on a woman. I thought they might ask how I’m feeling in my day-to-day life, like, "How are your feet?" or "Are you able to get enough rest while working full-time?"

Instead, those parts of my body are treated like mechanical components in a diagnostic check. It’s very transactional. I pull up my shirt, they measure, they listen, and then the exam is over. It’s always so fast. They focus on the belly as if it’s an object they’re auditing, rather than part of me. 

There’s a certain coldness to it that makes me want to pull away. When someone is touching you to measure your fundal height but they’re looking at a screen or a chart instead of looking at you, it makes me feel like my body isn't mine anymore; it's just a vessel for the clinical process. I feel like I’m standing there, exposed and vulnerable, and they’re looking *through* me. It makes the whole experience feel less like preparing for a baby and more like submitting to an inspection.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

It was during one of my recent appointments, right around the time my bump started to really "pop." I was already feeling particularly self-conscious—like my body was changing so fast I couldn’t quite keep up—and I went in for a routine check-up with a provider I hadn't seen before.

Without much warning or conversation, she just lifted my shirt and started pressing down quite firmly to check my positioning. It wasn’t that she was being overtly rough, but she didn’t explain what she was doing or ask if it was okay. She just sort of poked and prodded, commenting to her assistant about the "size of the uterus" and "firmness" as if I weren't even in the room. I felt like a piece of meat on a butcher's block.

I just stood there, staring at the wall, feeling this wave of humiliation. I wanted to say, "Hey, I’m a person, could you tell me what you’re doing?" but I felt paralyzed. I didn't want to seem like the difficult, emotional single girl. But that lack of basic consideration—just asking for permission or explaining the touch—it made me feel so small. I left that appointment feeling like my body was something I had surrendered to the state, and I cried in the car for twenty minutes before I could even start the engine.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven’t had to deal with a high-risk diagnosis—my midwives keep telling me my blood pressure is "textbook" and my urine screens are always clear. But honestly, the *possibility* of something going wrong is what keeps me awake at night.

Because I’m doing this alone, I think about those conditions as if they’re monsters waiting in the wings. I’ve spent so much time reading about gestational diabetes and pre-eclampsia online, mostly because I’m terrified that if something *did* go wrong, I’d be the last to know. My expectation was that if I were to develop something, the medical team would wrap their arms around me and guide me through it. 

But seeing how they handle my routine, healthy appointments—where I feel like a number—I’m scared that if I *did* become high-risk, I’d just be a "more complicated" number. I worry that the lack of warmth would turn into a lack of support. If I can't even get someone to look me in the eye when things are fine, how are they going to handle me when I’m actually, truly vulnerable? I feel like the system is set up for efficiency, not for someone who needs extra care, and that thought terrifies me more than the clinical complications themselves. I feel like I'm already managing a "risk" that no one else sees—the risk of being completely alone.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I felt something was wrong—even if it was just a strange cramp or a moment of panic—I could get a straight answer, quickly. I thought the medical system would be like a safety net. I figured if I called, there’d be someone who knew my history and could tell me, "Basilia, that’s normal, don't worry," or "You need to come in."

The reality is so much more frustrating. When I call with a question, I’m almost always funneled into a general nurse line or a message portal. I have to wait hours, sometimes an entire day, for a callback. And when they finally do reach out, it’s rarely someone who knows me. It’s a stranger who reads off a script, checking boxes about my symptoms to see if I meet the criteria for an "emergency." 

It makes me feel like I’m a nuisance for being worried. They don't seem to realize that for a single parent, every little twinge feels like a potential catastrophe because there is no one else at home to share the weight of that worry. Every time I get a generic, dismissive answer, I feel more isolated. I’ve stopped calling unless it’s absolutely unavoidable, which just means I spend more time alone in my apartment, terrified and Googling symptoms in the dark. The "safety net" feels more like a wall.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I grew up with this storybook idea that a pregnancy—especially a first one—brings everyone together. I expected my mother to be there, or at least a circle of friends, constantly checking in, rubbing my back, and asking about the baby. I thought the community would rally, that I’d be surrounded by this soft, protective bubble. 

The reality has been much quieter. People in Holden... they’re polite, but they keep their distance. Being never married, at my age, it’s like there’s this unspoken barrier. Some of my friends have drifted away entirely; they don't know how to talk to me anymore, or they're just in a different place in their lives. They see the "single mom" label and don't know what to do with it, so they just kind of stay away.

My family tries, in their own way, but it’s mostly through awkward text messages that don't really touch the surface of how I’m actually doing. There’s no one coming over to help me paint the nursery or to just sit with me so I’m not staring at the walls. 

It’s a heavy, crushing kind of realization. I’m the one doing the heavy lifting, the one making the appointments, the one handling the bills, and the one feeling every single kick. I’ve had to learn how to be my own community, which is exhausting. It’s hard to build a "soft" life when you’re doing all the hardening yourself just to get through the day.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think what I expected most was for people to just show up—to come over, maybe bring dinner, or just sit on the couch and talk about the future so I didn't have to face the quiet by myself. I expected that "village" everyone talks about, especially given how small a town Holden is. But that support just… it didn't materialize. It’s like people are waiting for an invitation I’m too proud or too tired to send, or maybe they just don’t want to witness the parts of my life that aren't picture-perfect.

What I *didn't* expect, and what has been so strange, is the support that’s come from total strangers online. I joined these forums for expectant mothers, and there are women there—people I’ll never meet—who are the only ones who truly get it. They don't judge the "never married" part or the fact that I’m working full-time while trying to grow a human. They’re the ones who reassure me at 2:00 AM when I’m panicking about a kick count.

It’s heartbreaking that the people who *should* be there, the ones who know my face and my name, are the ones who are absent. I’m grateful for the online support, truly, but a message on a screen can’t hold my hand or help me get the nursery furniture up the stairs. It’s a bittersweet trade-off: I have strangers across the country who understand my heart, but I’m still walking through the doors of the clinic alone.

I’ve been thinking about what it’s like to be "seen." My coworkers know I’m pregnant, of course, but it feels like they treat it like a temporary inconvenience I’m working through, rather than a whole life shifting on its axis. When my back hurts or I’m exhausted after a long shift, I see them look at each other with that "oh, she’s so young" expression, and it makes me want to scream. I’m doing this because I have to. There’s no one else.

I think the hardest part is that I’m trying so hard to be the "perfect" patient and the "perfect" employee, because if I slip up—if I show them I’m actually terrified—then I’m just fulfilling a stereotype. I have to prove to everyone, including my medical team, that I can handle this solo. But it’s exhausting to have to prove your worthiness while your body is busy building a person. 

I’m in my second trimester now, and the doctors say I’m in the "honeymoon phase." But I just feel like I’m in a holding pattern. I’m staring at the ceiling at night, wondering what it’s actually going to be like when the baby arrives. Will they look at me in the hospital and see a mother, or will they see a twenty-one-year-old girl who made a mistake? I just want someone to look at me and say, "You're going to be a great mom." Not because it's a script, but because they actually see how much I'm pouring into this. I’m tired of being a data point. I just want to be a person.

You know, it’s funny—I’ve spent so much time wishing for someone to just stop and really *see* me, but I also find myself hiding. It’s a strange contradiction. In the waiting room, I pull my cardigan tight over my bump, almost like I’m protecting it from the public eye. I’m scared that if I show too much, someone will ask me a question I can’t answer, or they’ll give me that pitying look that makes me feel smaller than I already do.

I find myself thinking about the delivery room a lot lately. I wonder who’s going to be there on shift. Will it be someone who understands that I don't have a partner to hold my hand? Will they be patient if I’m scared, or will they be counting the minutes until their shift ends? I have this nightmare that I’ll be in the middle of the most intense, life-changing experience of my life, and I’ll look over and see an empty chair. The silence of that room... that’s what I’m preparing for now. 

I’m trying so hard to build a "soft" life. I’ve picked out the paint colors—a soft, warm cream—and I’ve bought the softest blankets, trying to compensate for the hardness of the world around me. I want the baby to feel loved and secure, even if I have to be the one to provide all of that security by myself. I just hope that when the time comes, I’m enough. I’m doing all this reading, all this preparing, just to make sure I’m enough. I just wish I knew that someone else believed it, too.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

I think that question is a little complicated for me, given my situation. It’s strange, but sometimes the pressure I feel doesn't come from a partner, but from the *absence* of one. 

There wasn't a partner there to consult about the timing or the lifestyle changes, which honestly made the decisions feel heavier, not lighter. When you have a partner, even if you argue about it, there’s a shared weight of responsibility. With me, every single choice—like keeping my full-time job, skipping certain activities, or even the way I’m planning the nursery—rests entirely on my shoulders. I felt the pressure to be "perfectly responsible" so that no one could turn around and say, "Well, she was young and did this on her own, so what did she expect?" 

I’ve had to be my own partner in every sense. I’ve had to weigh the risks of working long hours against the need for money, and I’ve had to advocate for myself in every medical visit because I didn't have anyone else to ask the follow-up questions I was too nervous to voice. 

Sometimes, I think I would have preferred the friction of a disagreement with someone else, just to have another person involved in the life-altering decisions. Instead, it’s just this constant, internal monologue of "Is this the right thing for the baby?" and "Am I doing enough?" It’s a very solitary kind of pressure. No one else is deciding anything, which leaves me with the terrifying feeling that if I make the wrong choice, there’s no one else to blame—or to share the load.

Sometimes, I think about the life I’m building for this little one, and it feels like I’m sketching out a map in the dark. I worry that the lack of a "village" here in Holden is going to leave us both feeling exposed, even after the birth. It’s strange—I’ve spent so much time reading about fetal lung development and anatomy scans just to feel like I’m in control, but I know that once the baby is actually here, I can’t "study" my way through a fever or a sleepless night. 

I really hope that when the time comes to finally meet my baby, the hospital staff treats us with a bit of grace. I don't want to be "the young single mom in room four." I want to be *Basilia*, someone who is doing the work of two people and doing it with everything she has. 

Sometimes I talk to the baby at night, just to fill the quiet. I tell them that it’s just us, but that "just us" is enough. I’m trying to make the house feel soft and safe, even if my own life feels a bit like a storm. I’m constantly looking for a sign that I’m on the right track, that I’m capable. I just keep hoping that one day, when I walk into that clinic, someone will finally look me in the eye and see the person beneath the blood pressure reading. That’s all I really want. To be seen.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

It’s the comments that masquerade as "advice" that sting the most. Because I’m young, people—sometimes even the clinic staff—act as though I don’t know how to feed myself or keep my body healthy. They’ll make these offhand remarks about my weight gain or what I’m eating, framing it like a warning. They don't know that I’m working full-time, often skipping meals or grabbing whatever is fast because I’m trying to keep my head above water financially. They see a number on a chart and decide I’m being "irresponsible" or "negligent."

The worst was during a routine weigh-in. The provider looked at my chart, frowned, and said something like, "We really need to watch that weight, we don't want you getting too big too fast." It felt like a condemnation. It wasn't a conversation about health; it was a judgment on my discipline. They don't see the exhaustion, the stress, or the fact that I’m doing the work of two parents every single day. 

When they talk to me that way, I feel like a child, not an expectant mother. It makes me want to withdraw even further. I’m already carrying so much internal shame about being a single mom in this town; having that echoed back to me by the people who are supposed to be my healthcare team just makes the silence in my life feel heavier. It makes me feel like my body isn't a miracle—it’s just a problem to be managed.

It’s exhausting, really, how often I have to switch between being a "patient" and being a "person." When I’m at the clinic, I feel like I have to be the perfect student—patient, quiet, compliant—so they don’t get frustrated with me or dismiss my concerns as "drama." But then I walk out, and the weight of being twenty-one and facing this entire future on my own comes crashing back down.

I think about the delivery room constantly. I’ve started imagining it like a test I’m going to fail if I don’t memorize the right answers or stay calm enough. I’m scared that when the monitors start beeping and the nurses are rushing around, they won't see me as a mother fighting for her child, but just as a young girl who’s out of her depth. 

I just want to be treated with a little bit of grace. I want someone to ask, "How are *you* doing, Basilia?" and actually wait for the answer. I want them to look at the nursery I’ve built—the little cream-colored rug, the soft blankets, the books I’ve lined up—and see that I’ve poured every bit of my soul into making this home safe. I’m not just a data point in their system. I’m a person, and I’m doing the hardest, most important thing I’ll ever do. I just wish I didn't have to do it feeling so invisible.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I turn to the internet before I turn to anyone here in Holden. It’s almost a ritual at this point—I get a weird cramp, or I feel a movement that seems different, and instead of calling the clinic and bracing myself for a curt, hurried nurse on the phone, I pull up my phone and start searching. 

I’ve found these groups of other women—some in their twenties, some older, some who are doing this on their own just like me. They don’t see me as a “risk factor” or a “statistical concern.” They see me as someone who is scared. When I post a question, I get real answers back—not medical jargon, but human experiences. They tell me, “I felt that too, it’s okay,” or “Here’s what helped me when I was alone at night.” It’s the only place where I don’t feel like I have to perform or keep my voice steady.

It’s sad, really, that my "village" is made of usernames and profile pictures. I’ve never seen their faces, but they’ve seen more of my vulnerability than my own doctor has. They’re the ones who hold the space for me that my physical life doesn’t seem to have room for. They don't judge the lack of a partner; they just offer validation. Without those forums, I think I would have spiraled months ago. I’m leaning on them to keep me steady because the medical system feels like it’s just ticking boxes while I’m trying to keep a heart beating in the dark.

I worry about what happens when the monitor is turned off. When I finally deliver and I’m back in that quiet apartment, without a partner to share the middle-of-the-night feedings or to just hand the baby to when I’m at my breaking point—that’s the real fear. The medical side of things is one thing, but the isolation is a shadow that follows me everywhere. 

I’ve been practicing being “enough” in my head. I tell myself that I am all this baby needs, and for the most part, I believe it. But sometimes, especially after a long, lonely day at work where I feel like I’m hiding my bump behind my uniform, I just want someone to reach out and say, "I see you, and you’re doing a good job." 

I don't think the clinic realizes that every time they ignore my questions or rush me out the door, they’re reinforcing the idea that I’m alone in this. It’s like they’re confirming my worst fear—that I’m an island, and nobody is coming to bridge the gap. I’m doing my best to make our home a sanctuary, with the soft blankets and the quiet, steady rhythm of my own voice, but I’m just so tired of the silence. I’m ready to be a mother, truly I am, but I just wish someone—anyone—would act like I’m not doing it in a vacuum.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It’s rarely a conversation, to be honest. It’s almost always a delivery of information. When I go in for my appointments, there’s this unspoken script: the provider measures, interprets the results, and then tells me what to expect next. It’s not that they’re being mean, but it feels like there’s no room for me to be an active participant in my own care. 

I’ve never once had someone ask me, “What are your preferences for the birth?” or “What do you need from us to feel safer during the labor process?” It’s always “We’ll do this at the next scan” or “You’re on track for this type of delivery.” I’m treated like I’m a passenger in my own pregnancy, sitting in the backseat while they drive. 

I think the biggest missed opportunity is that they never ask about my life outside those four walls. If they knew how much I struggle with the isolation, or how terrified I am of that silence in the delivery room, they might approach things differently. Instead, I’m just told what’s going to happen. It’s a very passive way to experience something so fundamental. I’m the one who’s going to go through the labor, and I’m the one who will be raising this child alone, yet I feel like my voice is the least important part of the entire process. I’m just the host, and they’re the ones in charge.

You know, even saying all this out loud—that I feel like a passenger in my own life—makes me wonder if I'm being too sensitive. But that’s the trap, isn’t it? I’ve spent months training myself to be this invisible, "low-risk" patient, and now it feels like I’ve actually disappeared. 

I think about the day I’ll finally bring the baby home to Holden. I have this fear that the isolation I’m feeling now is just a preview of the rest of my life. The clinic doesn't see that. They see a young woman with good blood pressure and a healthy-sized fetus. They don't see the woman who had to choose between extra shifts and buying a better crib, or the one who sits in her car and cries because the silence of her apartment feels like a physical weight.

I just want to be seen as more than the sum of my medical records. I want someone to acknowledge that being twenty-one and doing this on my own is a monumental task, and that I'm doing it well. If they could just bridge that gap—if they could just ask me how I’m *really* doing, or even just pause long enough to look at me instead of my chart—maybe then the future wouldn't feel so incredibly lonely. I’m doing all the heavy lifting, but I’m still just waiting for someone to notice.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was a moment about a month ago, around my anatomy scan. I was so nervous—not just about the baby’s health, but because it felt like a milestone I was crossing without a partner to witness it. I’d spent all week mentally preparing, writing down questions in a little notebook about what to look for and what "normal" growth meant for me.

When I got into the room, the technician was very focused, but she didn’t say a word to me. She was cold, just moving the wand and tapping at her keyboard. I finally worked up the courage to ask, "Does everything look okay? Is the heart rhythm where it should be?" She didn’t even look up from the screen; she just said, "The doctor will discuss the results with you later," and kept working in total silence.

In that moment, I felt like a stranger in my own body. It was like I was just a piece of equipment they were operating on. I walked out of that clinic feeling so small, realizing that for them, this was just a scan, just another patient, just another file. But for me? It was the moment I was supposed to see my baby, and instead, I was made to feel like I was a nuisance for wanting to be included in the process. It shattered the illusion that I was being cared for. I realized then that I wasn’t a person to them—I was just a task to be completed before their lunch break. I haven't looked at the staff the same way since.

It’s the silence that lingers the most after those moments. You leave the office, and you’re back in the parking lot in Holden, sitting in your car, and it’s like the world has suddenly become much colder. You were just hoping for a flicker of recognition, a simple "Everything looks strong, you're doing great," but instead, you get a clinical dismissal that leaves you wondering if you’re doing something wrong by simply existing in that space.

I keep thinking about the delivery room again. If I can't get a kind word or a moment of clarity from a technician during a simple scan, how am I going to find support when I’m actually in pain? When I’m at my absolute most vulnerable? The trust just isn’t there, and that’s a dangerous thing to feel when you’re about to go through the most intense, life-altering experience of your life. 

I’m trying to stay strong. I’m trying to keep the "honeymoon phase" going for the sake of the baby, and I’m trying to keep the nursery feeling like a sanctuary. But deep down, I feel like I’m standing on a foundation of sand. I just wish I could walk into that clinic and feel like they had my back—not just the back of the fetus, not just the back of a medical file, but *mine*. Because I’m the one who has to carry us both forward. I’m the one who has to be the village. And it’s just so heavy sometimes.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

It’s in the quiet spaces, the ones that aren't quite medical but are still part of the appointment. Like the waiting room—I look at the other women, mostly in their late twenties or thirties, with someone sitting next to them. They’re sharing a look when they hear the heartbeat, or laughing when the baby moves, or just leaning into each other when the wait gets long. I’m always, always alone. I have to be the one to hear the heartbeat, hold the excitement, and absorb the anxiety all by myself. There’s no one to turn to for that shared "Did you see that?" moment. 

But it’s worse in the exam room. When the midwife asks, "Any questions?" and I’m sitting there, trying to process my own health, the baby’s development, the financial pressure, and the sheer terror of doing this solo—I freeze. If there were two of us, one could focus on the clinical answers while the other watched the screen. By myself, I have to be the observer, the advocate, and the parent all at once. 

When I leave the clinic, I’m the only one who has to carry the information back to the empty apartment. I don’t get to debrief. I don't get to hand the weight over to someone else. I just walk back into the silence, and that’s when the reality of being single really hits—it’s not just about who’s at the birth; it’s about having no one to share the small, heavy reality of the journey. The absence of a partner isn't a missing person; it's a missing voice, a missing hand, a missing half of the experience. It makes the world feel infinitely larger and lonelier.

I feel like I'm balancing on a tightrope, and below me is just silence. Sometimes, I look at the clothes I’ve folded for the baby—tiny, soft things—and I feel this wave of fierce, sharp love. But then I catch my reflection in the window, and I see myself: young, tired, and completely on my own. It’s such a strange, jarring contrast. I know I’m capable, I really do, but why does the world have to make it feel so heavy?

When I walk into the clinic next time, I’ll probably put on the same mask. I’ll be the compliant, low-risk, polite girl who doesn't take up too much space. But I’m so tired of being that person. I want to tell them that my blood pressure is fine because I’m *trying* to stay calm, not because I actually *am* calm. I’m holding my breath for this entire pregnancy, just waiting for the moment when I don't have to be the only one holding the line.

I don't know what’s going to happen when I finally go into labor. I have this recurring, quiet terror that when the time comes, I’ll be looking for a familiar face, a hand to squeeze, and I’ll just be looking at a wall. I keep telling the baby, "It’s us. Just us." And I hope that’s enough. I think it has to be. I just wish that for once, someone in that white coat would look at me and actually see the mother I’m becoming, rather than the "patient" who’s just passing through.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single time I have a question that isn't about a raw, clinical number, I go online. It’s not because I don’t know *how* to find medical information—I read the textbooks, I look at the research—it’s because I’m looking for the *human* side of the data. 

I’m looking for someone to tell me what a certain pain feels like in practice, not just in a list of symptoms. I’m looking for people who can talk about the terror of being the only one holding the baby when they have a fever, or how to juggle a full-time job when you’re utterly exhausted and your body feels like it’s failing you. When I ask my midwife if I’m "doing okay," she gives me a clinical "Yes, your vitals are perfect." That’s not what I’m asking! I’m asking if *I* am okay, if this struggle is normal, if the fear of being alone is something other women navigate.

I search for threads on being a single mother because I need to hear that I’m not a failure. I look for stories about labor that aren't just "the baby was born healthy," but stories where someone talks about being alone and finding a way through the panic. I’m hunting for a map that shows me how to be a parent when you don't have a partner, because the medical world treats "single" like a box you check on a form, not a reality that changes every single thing about how you experience this transition.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think... I just want people to know that I am not a "case." 

When I walk into that clinic in Holden, I am terrified, but I am also brave. I’m working full-time, I’m reading the books, I’m prepping the nursery, and I’m doing it all while holding the weight of the future entirely by myself. Every time a nurse or a midwife treats me like a line item on a spreadsheet, they’re missing the fact that I’m standing right in front of them, building a life from nothing.

I don’t want to be "managed." I want to be witnessed. I want to feel that for the one hour I’m in that office, someone is actually on my side—not just on the side of the fetus, but on the side of the woman who is working so hard to get them here. 

I’m scared of the silence in the delivery room, and I’m scared of the exhaustion that’s coming. But more than anything, I’m scared of becoming so numb to the clinical, sterile nature of all this that I forget how to feel the joy of it. I want to be more than a patient. I want to be a mother. And I really hope that when the time comes, someone in that room remembers that I’m a human being, and that I’m doing the most important thing of my life—alone. Thank you for listening. It feels... better, just to say it.
